paragraphs.body: Games That’s Outside of the Window

annotations.comment: Something to think about throughout this essay: make sure your verb tenses stay consistent. It's an English-language rule that isn't the same in Chinese.
annotations.context_left: Games That’s Outside of the Window

paragraphs.body: The breeze brushed against my cheeks, and I closed my eyes and felt the smell of rain flowing in the air. The moon is already out, and the bright moonlight shines on the road ahead like the sun. Every blade of grass swayed with the wind, and insects fluttered beside the faintly glowing garden lights. “Finally home”, I said. Nobody is answering me, and yet, that’s what I want. The car which is usually parked in our parking lot is gone. I took a deep breath, walking passing the flowers and grass, a smile gradually appeared on my face as I walked into the house with my keys in hand. “Mom?”, I yelled at the empty house, but no one replied. Yes, yes, yes, my heart started beating. Finally, I can be left alone! I quickly ran passing the stairs with the humming coming out of my throat. I closed my door and opened my computer. Finally, no one can stop me from playing video games. My hands were dancing on the keyboard, as my eyes stuck to the screen. Video games are stuck in my brain. I can see nothing else but games on the screen. The galaxy, the planets, adventures in abandoned factories, traveling through the ever-changing starry night sky, everything that cannot be seen, appeared in front of me so easily.

annotations.text: Yes, yes, yes, my heart started beating.
Every blade of grass swayed with the wind, and insects fluttered beside the faintly glowing garden lights.

annotations.comment: Wow! What a lovely descriptive paragraph :)

paragraphs.body: “I can look everywhere. I have video game hands. My face doesn’t exist. I don’t need anything else…

Except hands. For grasping.” Flashing back to when I was young, I never played video games before, but how does it come into my life? I think the very first time I interacted with it was in 6th grade. The animations and the diversity of different gameplay shaded this whole new world and gave me an unforgettable memory. As I discovered this new path, my life had completely changed by it, a little and a little, and shaded my family and me. All my siblings play video games, and the same with 80 percent of my friends. Gaming is the language I speak with those I am familiar with. It is a language of entertainment. People who don’t play video games cannot understand it. Speaking these languages is like eating food to me. It also acts like a string and connects me with others. We often talk about different characters in the game, and many hilarious memes that outsiders can’t understand why it’s funny. We will also talk about things like “crit rate”, “crit damage”, and “Hp”. When we talk about roaming, we do not mean “moving about aimlessly or unsystematically, especially over a wide area” (google). For us, roaming means to support other players from place to place when they are needed. Sometimes we can be hypocrites too. For example, we say “”GG” (meaning good game), when we think it’s a great match or criticize a teammate for being so trashy. Additionally, if you are a sports person, and you hear us saying “Cooldown or just simply CD”, we do not mean the walking or slowly jogging for five to ten minutes after exercise. We meant the amount of time you need to wait until you can use certain skills again.

annotations.text: Speaking these languages is like eating food to me. It also acts like a string and connects me with others.
annotations.comment: Interesting. Is this connection more important to you than other connections? Does it replace other connections? Let's talk :)
excellent concrete and specific details and language throughout this paragraph!
annotations.context_left: unsystematically, especially over a wide area” (google). For us, roaming means to

paragraphs.body: "Stop playing video games." is a saying I heard often growing up. Ever since I got addicted to them, this quote never goes away. For me, video games are interesting and help me relax. But my mom is a typical Chinese parent who hates their kids being on their phones and believes that video games are the reason for everything, such as affect grades even when I got all A's in my classes. She gave me a restriction and screen time because she thought it was bad for my eyes and I played too much. However I believe that video games only have a little effect on my eyes, and the main reason for my eyes to be this bad is studying. Especially when I was a kid, I read books when the lights were off. I had bad eyesight since I was in 3rd grade, but when I told my mom that I couldn’t see the board, she did not believe me until 6th grade when the eye doctor told her I needed glasses and also when I started playing video games. Now, she blames everything on my phone and always says to me,” I wish I never buy you a phone.” She always watches me when I’m playing, which makes me very uncomfortable. She also kept yelling at me for being on my phone longer than 6 hours each week and kept asking me how long I had been playing. As a result, I started my first “phone war” with her, and this was the first time I lied - about video games.

annotations.text: As a result, I started my first “phone war” with her,
annotations.comment: I'm interested, but what do you mean?

paragraphs.body: “There are no mirrors in here. There are no mirrors in this world. I’d rather just be hands. Not a face.” I saw her crying, saying how my phone turned me from a good daughter into this rebellious kid. I know I’m making her sad, I don’t want to, but I need to stand up for myself once. Everyone around me can be as long as they want to be on their phone, but why can’t I? Even my brothers are less restrictive than me, and I kept telling myself it was not fair. As a always obedient kid, I told myself I have to be mean once, or else I knew I would never be able to have a phone again. And yet, the war begins with me refusing to stop playing games. She tore down my door and put the camera in my room just to prevent me from playing games on my phone. She sets me on fire, I start playing around with my computer and learning some coding skills so I can play games secretly while leaving no history behind. I started changing the app logo and name and using different methods to hide games into different files and tables. I acted like I didn’t know anything about computers in front of her just trying to give her the illusion that I’m a computer idiot, so she wouldn’t check my computer so closely. Furthermore, I discovered ways to unlock screen time and delete game time showing on my iPhone. I also irritated her by saying she couldn’t stop me from playing games as long as I wanted to. I could see her frustration because she tried to catch evidence of me playing games, but she couldn’t. She asked me how I did all this because she knew I was playing, but I refused to tell her. Things got worse when she confiscated my phone, and expected me to get all A’s while I can’t play games to relax, which led me to the idea of leaving the home.

annotations.text: She tore down my door and put the camera in my room just to prevent me from playing games on my phone. She sets me on fire, I start playing around with my computer and learning some coding skills so I can play games secretly
“There are no mirrors in here. There are no mirrors in this world. I’d rather just be hands. Not a face.”
annotations.comment: Fab repetition!  I'm also very interested in the potential metaphor of the mirror. What do you mean? It seems you don't want to "look" at yourself for some reason. How might this relate to connection, or to your relationship with your mother?

annotations.text: which led me to the idea of leaving the home.

annotations.comment: This is so fascinating. I wonder, again, if the video games are a metaphor. Is there a way that they represent you breaking free from something? Let's talk :)
annotations.context_right: just to prevent me from playing games on my phone. She sets me on fire, I start playing

paragraphs.body: My friends in China have the same problem as me, but they are worse. This summer I went to China for vacation. I met my friends and hung out for a bit. Shockingly, I noticed that their parents put them into a training class, in which they do nothing but eat, study, and sleep every single day during summer break. No break, but continuous learning and doing problems in the book. My friend’s whole face has countless red aces, rough, and bumpy skin like a mutated monster. He told me he gave up his life outside of the classroom, studying endlessly, and finally, he got into a top 10 college in China. I know there was a lot of stress according to social media, but didn’t know it was that crazy. Meanwhile, I have another friend who was not able to hang out with us when both our families had a reunion, and can’t even attend her mother’s birthday party because she had outside school classes every day from day to night, even on the weekend. She complains to me that how her mother locked her phone and she can’t even chat with her friend more than an half hour a day.

“It always starts the same. With three chances. With three choices. I try my best every time.” Not only that, many people in the world also like them. All we want is a break from reality, but do video games really hurt our lives? For me yes but no, it does cause me not to want to go out, but somehow causes me to have an interest in computer science and I can gain happiness instead of being depressed when I’m not able to go out (such as COVID quarantine). I also earned a lot from the games. I learned new languages and became more and more creative as I saw different phenomena with my eyes in the game. Games can be real too, they give you emotion and teach you lessons about the world without hurting physically.

annotations.comment: I'm very interested by the way you use poetry as a structural motif.

paragraphs.body: In fact, especially in China, parents want their kids to be successful, but many parents do not care about their children’s happiness. Most Chinese parents are very strict on their kids, because they want their kid to be the top one in their class. They beat their kids when they can’t get an A on a test. They dehumanzied their kids for being “school idiot”. Meanwhile, the school also gives them a lot of stress because everyone there knows grades are everything for their life. If they can’t get a good grade on the Gaokao (college entry exam), their life is ruined. No college wants them, and they have nowhere to go, nowhere to stay. Family is not a home for them anymore, because they know all they will be facing are yelling and disappointment. The only time they can take a break is through gaming, but what if this is also got taken away? What should they do? Suicide and depression is not a rare thing to see anymore. As time passed, high expectations of their parents and fierce competition with their peers, create this atmosphere that blocks many potential world changers into an endless abyss. They go straight to the window and jump right down without hesitating. Maybe these kids just want to punish their parents using their death. They want to see the regret of yelling and beating on their face. What if you just teach the kid the right way to control their phone instead of being so thorny?

annotations.text: In fact, especially in China, parents want their kids to be successful, but many parents do not care about their children’s happiness. Most Chinese parents are very strict on their kids,
annotations.comment: Might this be the attitude that you want to break away from? That you're, in a sense, "escaping"?

annotations.text: go straight to the window and jump right down without hesitating. Maybe these kids just want to punish their parents using their death.

paragraphs.body: “Beep beep” a very small noise caught my ear. I know what is coming. I quickly stand up and look out the window. They are back, I have to be quick. I rush out my homework and place them all over the desk. Close up the tabs and open Google Classroom. I open my door and briskly sit back down and pretend I’m a good kid who is just studying. I don’t remember how many times I did this, and how many times I tricked my mom and pretending I was doing my homework, pretending that I was her “nerd” daughter who loved school. I can never go back to that girl who is more excited about the outside world than a single laptop. But this is me. My culture and my language. And my “language” shaded my life.
